# Design Taste Translation

## Summary

One of the biggest recurring failures in LLM-driven frontend work is not implementation skill but taste translation. The agent can generate working UI while still missing the intended feel, the user task, and the intended atmosphere.

## Core Problem

Agents often know how to produce:

- valid JSX
- responsive layouts
- component structure
- animations

But they often fail at converting taste into:

- hierarchy
- restraint
- rhythm
- emphasis
- composition
- brand-appropriate atmosphere
- task-appropriate typography
- deliberate motion

## Hard Rule

For visual work, do not start from generic UI patterns.

Start from one of:

- repo-local design system
- user-provided reference
- a maintained frontend overlay
- an explicit visual direction
- a documented taste system

Before building a page or major component, write a short taste brief in plain language.

If a maintained overlay already matches the requested direction, start there and adapt only what the repo or user clearly changes.

For professional product websites without a stronger local design system, start from [[Frontend Overlay - Polished Product Website]]. Use the more specific [[Frontend Overlay - Linear Inspired B2B SaaS]] only when the user asks for Linear-like dark product-led B2B SaaS direction.

## Taste Brief

The agent should be able to state:

- what the user is trying to do: focused task work, scanning, reading, selling, browsing, or mixed
- the focal point of the view
- the typography mode: more productive, more expressive, or intentionally blended
- the spacing rhythm and alignment logic
- the surface treatment: flat, layered, editorial, dense, minimal, etc.
- the color model: dominant base, accent behavior, restraint rules
- the motion model: subtle/productive, expressive, or nearly static
- the primitive stack: token roles, typography ladder, section shell, and control family
- the anti-goals: what would make the UI feel wrong or generic
- the verification gate: which screenshots and checks will prove the result is not sloppy

## What The Agent Must Extract Before Coding

- typography direction
- typography ladder
- spacing rhythm
- layout logic
- emphasis pattern
- surface treatment
- token roles
- control sizing and state treatment
- motion behavior
- what should be avoided

## Translate Style Into Primitives

Taste is not usable at scale until it becomes a small system.

- convert colors into semantic roles, not just a palette dump
- convert typography into named display and text roles, not isolated font sizes
- convert layout into a repeated section shell with clear container and spacing rules
- convert buttons, inputs, and pills into a control family with consistent radii, heights, borders, and focus states
- convert motion into named speeds and easing rules instead of scattered timings

If this translation cannot be stated clearly, the agent still has inspiration, not a system.

If the agent cannot state these clearly, it should not build the whole page yet.

## What Usually Goes Wrong

- too many equal-weight cards
- weak type hierarchy
- generic centered hero layouts
- overuse of borders, badges, or icons
- decorative motion without meaning
- "premium" effects used as filler instead of structure
- decorative blobs, bokeh, gradients, and glow used as visual filler
- too many borders around objects that should be separated by spacing or type hierarchy
- muddy or oversized shadows used to create fake depth
- misaligned buttons, labels, controls, or media edges
- productive task UI rendered with oversized editorial styling
- expressive marketing UI rendered with cramped product typography
- mixed typography modes inside one small region without a clear hierarchy reason
- spacing that drifts off-scale and weakens rhythm

## Practical Rule

If the agent cannot describe the visual system in plain language before coding, it probably does not understand the taste well enough yet.

Keep typography styles consistent within a discrete task, component, or region unless a deliberate contrast is part of the hierarchy.

## Best Skill Pairings

- see [[Skill Routing Catalog]] for the primary frontend chooser
- `reference-to-system`: preferred when the agent needs to turn references into an explicit design system before coding
- `design-taste-frontend`: exception path for taste diagnosis only, not the default build route
- [[Frontend Quality Gate]]: use before handoff to verify the rendered result
